name: Message Queues
description: Enable asynchronous communication between services by decoupling producers from consumers. Absorb traffic spikes and enable fault tolerance.
keyPoints: Kafka: Distributed log, high throughput, message retention, replay capability
RabbitMQ: AMQP protocol, flexible routing, message deleted after consumption
Consumer groups enable parallel processing across partitions
Dead Letter Queues capture messages that fail processing
Idempotency is critical: same message may be delivered multiple times
AWS SQS: Managed serverless queue with FIFO and standard modes
whenToUse: Kafka for event streaming, log aggregation. RabbitMQ for task queues, complex routing. SQS for AWS ecosystem.
interviewQuestions: What is the difference between Kafka and RabbitMQ?
What are delivery semantics (at-least-once, exactly-once)?
How do you handle message ordering in a distributed queue?
What is a dead letter queue?

name: Database Sharding
description: Horizontal partitioning that splits a large database into smaller shards distributed across multiple servers.
keyPoints: Hash-based sharding: Even distribution but range queries are expensive
Range-based sharding: Efficient for range queries but can create hotspots
Directory-based sharding: Flexible but adds latency
Shard key selection is critical for even distribution
Cross-shard queries are expensive and complex
Consistent hashing minimizes data movement during rebalancing
whenToUse: When single DB can't handle write throughput or data volume exceeds single-node capacity.
interviewQuestions: What is database sharding vs replication?
How do you choose a good shard key?
What problems arise with cross-shard queries?
How do you handle adding a new shard?

name: Database Replication
description: Creates multiple copies of data across nodes to improve availability, read performance, and fault tolerance.
keyPoints: Leader-Follower: Writes to leader, reads from replicas; simple but leader is SPOF for writes
Multi-Leader: Multiple nodes accept writes; needs conflict resolution
Leaderless (Quorum): Any node accepts reads/writes; R + W > N ensures consistency
Asynchronous: Leader writes and returns; risk of data loss
Synchronous: Stronger consistency but higher latency
Replication lag can cause stale reads
whenToUse: Read-heavy workloads, high availability needs, geographic distribution for low-latency reads.
interviewQuestions: What are different types of database replication?
How does replication lag affect consistency?
When would you choose synchronous vs asynchronous?
What is a quorum and how does it work?

name: Indexing
description: Data structure technique that speeds up data retrieval by creating shortcuts to data locations, turning O(n) scans into O(log n) lookups.
keyPoints: B-Tree index: Most common; efficient for equality and range queries
Hash index: O(1) for exact matches; cannot support range queries
Composite index: Column order matters for query optimization
Inverted index: Maps words to documents; used in full-text search
LSM-Tree: Write-optimized; used in Cassandra, RocksDB
Trade-off: Faster reads but slower writes, additional storage
whenToUse: Columns in WHERE, JOIN, ORDER BY clauses. High-read, low-write tables benefit most.
interviewQuestions: What is the difference between B-Tree and Hash indexes?
How does a composite index work?
What are trade-offs of too many indexes?
What is an inverted index?

name: Microservices vs Monolith
description: Monolith deploys as single unit. Microservices decompose into independent, loosely coupled services with separate data stores.
keyPoints: Monolith: Simpler initially, shared codebase, scales as a unit
Microservices: Independent deployment, technology diversity, team autonomy
Communication: Synchronous (REST, gRPC) or asynchronous (queues)
Challenges: Distributed transactions, network failures, debugging complexity
Saga pattern: Compensating transactions instead of 2-phase commit
Strangler Fig: Gradually replace monolith components
whenToUse: Monolith for small teams, early products. Microservices for large teams, complex domains, independent scaling needs.
interviewQuestions: What are pros and cons of microservices vs monolith?
When should you choose monolith over microservices?
How do you handle distributed transactions?
What is the Saga pattern?

name: CDN (Content Delivery Network)
description: Geographically distributed network of proxy servers that caches and delivers static content from edge locations close to users.
keyPoints: Edge servers cache content at locations near users
Push CDN: You push content to edges; good for stable content
Pull CDN: Edge fetches from origin on first request; good for dynamic content
Providers: Cloudflare, AWS CloudFront, Akamai, Fastly
Cache invalidation: TTL-based, manual purge, versioned URLs
Benefits: Reduced latency, bandwidth costs, DDoS protection
whenToUse: Static assets, video streaming, global applications, API response caching for read-heavy endpoints.
interviewQuestions: What is a CDN and how does it improve performance?
What is the difference between Push and Pull CDN?
How do you handle cache invalidation in a CDN?
Can a CDN cache dynamic content?

name: DNS (Domain Name System)
description: Translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses. Hierarchical, distributed naming system.
keyPoints: Resolution: Browser -> OS -> Recursive resolver -> Root -> TLD -> Authoritative
Record types: A (IPv4), AAAA (IPv6), CNAME (alias), MX (mail), TXT, NS
TTL: How long records are cached; lower TTL = faster propagation
DNS caching at multiple levels: Browser, OS, ISP
Geographic routing: Route users to nearest data center
DNS-based load balancing: Round-robin, weighted, latency-based
whenToUse: Always needed. Geographic routing, failover, load balancing, service discovery in microservices.
interviewQuestions: How does DNS resolution work step by step?
What is the difference between A, CNAME, and MX records?
How does DNS caching work at different levels?
How can DNS be used for load balancing?

name: Consensus Algorithms
description: Enable distributed systems to agree on a single value or state despite failures. Ensure consistency across multiple nodes.
keyPoints: Paxos: Foundational but complex; used in Google Chubby
Raft: Designed for understandability; leader-based; used in etcd, CockroachDB
Byzantine Fault Tolerance: Tolerates malicious nodes; expensive
Leader election: Designated node coordinates consensus
Log replication: Leader replicates to followers; majority must acknowledge
Two-Phase Commit (2PC): Coordinator asks prepare then commit; blocks if coordinator fails
whenToUse: Distributed databases needing strong consistency, leader election, configuration management, blockchain.
interviewQuestions: What is the difference between Paxos and Raft?
How does Raft achieve consensus?
What is Byzantine Fault Tolerance?
How does 2PC work and what are its limitations?
